search_terms_used: ACWR injury risk distance runners systematic review meta-analysis
training load monitoring endurance athletes running injury prevention
workload injury prevention running session RPE Foster training load
coverage_notes: Strong coverage of ACWR concept (origin, validation, critique). Two running-specific ACWR papers (Dijkhuis 2020, Cloosterman 2024). Most foundational ACWR work is in team sports (cricket, rugby league) — honestly flagged. Good coverage of both pro-ACWR and critical perspectives (Impellizzeri 2020). IOC consensus and monitoring consensus statements included.

papers.title: The training-injury prevention paradox: should athletes be training smarter and harder?
papers.notes: Proposed the training-injury prevention paradox and recommended maintaining ACWR within 0.8-1.3 across multiple sports.

papers.title: Spikes in acute workload are associated with increased injury risk in elite cricket fast bowlers
papers.notes: Foundational ACWR paper tracking 28 elite cricket fast bowlers over 6 years to show acute workload spikes increase injury risk.

papers.title: The acute:chronic workload ratio predicts injury: high chronic workload may decrease injury risk in elite rugby league players
papers.notes: Demonstrated that ACWR predicts injury in rugby league and that high chronic workloads are protective.

papers.title: Low chronic workload and the acute:chronic workload ratio are more predictive of injury than between-match recovery time: a two-season prospective cohort study in elite rugby league players
papers.notes: Prospective cohort of 28 rugby league players over two seasons comparing ACWR vs recovery time as injury predictors.

papers.title: How do training and competition workloads relate to injury? The workload-injury aetiology model
papers.notes: Proposed an updated injury aetiology model incorporating workloads through exposure, fatigue, and fitness mechanisms.

papers.title: Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio: Conceptual Issues and Fundamental Pitfalls
papers.journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
papers.notes: Critically examined ACWR's statistical properties and argued there is no causal evidence supporting its use for injury prevention.

papers.title: Acute to chronic workload ratio (ACWR) for predicting sports injury risk: a systematic review and meta-analysis
papers.journal: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation
papers.notes: Meta-analysis of 22 cohort studies evaluating ACWR effectiveness in predicting sports injuries using Newcastle-Ottawa Scale quality assessment.

papers.title: Increase in the Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio relates to Injury Risk in Competitive Runners
papers.notes: Prospective study tracking 23 competitive runners over 24 months to examine ACWR-injury relationship in running specifically.

papers.title: Comparison of Weekly Training Load and Acute: Chronic Workload Ratio Methods to Estimate Change in Training Load in Running
papers.notes: Compared weekly training load vs ACWR methods for estimating training load changes in 430 runners using 22,839 GPS-tracked sessions.

papers.title: The Association Between Changes in Weekly Running Distance and Running-Related Injury: Preparing for a Half Marathon
papers.journal: Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy
papers.notes: Prospective cohort of 261 half-marathon runners examining weekly distance changes and running-related injury over 14 weeks.

papers.title: How much is too much? (Part 1) International Olympic Committee consensus statement on load in sport and risk of injury
papers.notes: IOC consensus statement reviewing evidence on training and competition load as risk factors for injury across all sports.

papers.title: Monitoring Athlete Training Loads: Consensus Statement
papers.journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
papers.notes: Consensus statement from 2016 Doha conference providing a shared framework for training load monitoring concepts and methods.
